summary: Martin Fowler argues that remote-first teams, despite their many advantages, still need regular face-to-face gatherings to maintain effectiveness. While remote work excels for focused solo tasks, activities requiring rapid multi-person input are better done in person. The most valuable aspect of these gatherings isn't the scheduled work but the informal social interactions that build trust and human connection. He recommends week-long gatherings every two to three months for distributed teams, viewing travel costs as an investment rather than an expense to minimize.
key_points: Human interactions are always richer face-to-face; video calls tend toward transactional exchanges that miss the chitchat building genuine relationships
Tasks requiring input from many people with rapid feedback are much easier in person; remote tools cannot replicate the depth of same-room interaction
Trust is hard to develop online without social cues, making informal conversations during breaks more valuable than scheduled work sessions
Teams should meet for a week every 2-3 months, with at minimum two face-to-face meetings per year
Organizations should view gathering costs as investments in team effectiveness rather than expenses to cut
money_quotes: Human interactions are always richer when they are face-to-face.
Video calls too easily become transactional, with little time for the chitchat that builds a proper human relationship.
Without those deeper bonds, misunderstandings fester into serious relationship difficulties.
The most valuable part of a face-to-face gathering isn't the scheduled work, it's chitchat while getting a coffee, and conviviality over lunch.
Trust is hard to develop online, where there isn't the social cues that can happen when we are in the same room.
Informal conversations, mostly not about work, forge the human contact that makes the work interactions be more effective.
Compared to this, saving on airplanes and hotels is a false economy.
key_insight: Remote teams need periodic face-to-face gatherings not primarily for the work sessions, but for the informal social interactions that build the trust necessary for effective remote collaboration.
